retrieved_docs.title: Chemical Burns -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chemical burns are the result of exposure to a variety of substances commonly found at home, the workplace, and the surrounding environment. The burn may be obvious, for example, from a direct spill or other exposure, or more covert, especially in children. Chemical burns can cause short-term, long-term, and lifelong health problems, especially if undertreated. Occasionally, they can result in premature death, especially if ingested in an attempt to self-harm. [4] [5] Common causes of chemical burns include the following: Acids: Sulfuric, nitric, hydrofluoric, hydrochloric, acetic acid, formic, phosphoric, phenols, and chloroacetic acid Bases: Sodium and potassium hydroxide, calcium hydroxide, sodium and calcium hypochlorite, ammonia, phosphates, silicated, sodium carbonate, lithium hydride Oxidants: Bleaches like chlorites used in the home, peroxides, chromates, magnates Miscellaneous: White phosphorus, metals, hair coloring agents, airbag injuries Vesicants like mustard gas

retrieved_docs.title: Acute and Chronic Thermal Burn Evaluation and Management -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There is significant variation in burn wound care regimens, but all follow several basic principles. Adequate wound care serves to prevent infection, allows for slow mechanical removal of devitalized tissue, helps prevent fluid loss, and is cost-effective. Common classic topical antibacterial regimens from least to most tissue penetrance include silver nitrate, silver sulfadiazine, and mafenide acetate. The most commonly used is silver sulfadiazine as it has a good penetrance, good antibiotic spectrum against gram positives and negatives, and is less painful than mafenide acetate. Silver nitrate, with activity against Staph species and Pseudomonas, is generally used for superficial burns in patients with Sulfa allergies. Mafenide acetate, which is highly effective against gram negatives and has deep tissue penetrance, is generally reserved for very deep, heavily contaminated wounds, those with eschar, and auricular burns due to its superior cartilage penetration.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the location of the hydrofluoric acid exposure and the percentage of the acid, patients can present immediately with severe caustic burns to the body and severe pain, with pain out of proportion as the hallmark finding.  Solutions greater than 14.5% will produce immediate symptoms, 12% solutions can take up to an hour, and solutions less than 7% can take hours before symptoms occur.  The concern exists for ocular and respiratory injury when any chemical is splashed / sprayed in the face.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrofluoric acid exposure is a rare occurrence throughout the globe compared to other industrial injuries with an estimated 1000 cases annually, though actual incident rates are not known.  15 year study from Stuke et al showed an incident rate of 35 patients (17%) out of 204 chemical burns. [2] A 10-year study in China reported 690 patients with chemical burns and a little over half being involved with hydrofluoric acid and sulfuric acid.  Most of the chemical burns occurred in the summer and autumn seasons. [14] A 20-year survey from Taiwan only had 324 identified calls of hydrofluoric acid with the majority of those incidents being dermal in nature. [1] Because of the nature of the occupations with the greatest potential injury from and exposure to hydrofluoric acid exposure, the majority of patients are adult males.  Upper extremity location was most commonly involved. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Burns -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to one database, alkali injuries (53.6%) were seen more commonly than acid injuries (46.4%). [1] Some sources suggest alkali burns outnumber acid burns almost 2 to 1. Ammonia-containing compounds are the most common cause of alkali burns, while sulfuric acid is the most common cause of acid burns. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Chemical Burns -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Healthcare professionals should understand chemical burns from exposure to acids (pH less than 7), alkalis (pH greater than 7), and irritants to recognize, manage and care for these common types of injury. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Chemical Burns -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Because burns can occur on almost any part of the body, specific guidelines in the management of each organ system are lacking. However, there is expert evidence on managing the patient as a whole. However, there still remain several gaps in the early management of chemical burns. What solution to rinse the skin or the eye and when to debride are two issues that continue to be debated. But there is no debate that the eye should be rinsed thoroughly, and the patient must be seen by the ophthalmologist. Because burns can affect all organ systems an interprofessional approach with interaction is necessary to avoid the high morbidity of the disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrogen Sulfide Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The combustible nature of hydrogen sulfide gas increases the risk of explosions; hence, burns and traumatic injuries are additional concerns. All affected individuals should be assessed for acute traumatic injuries if they survive toxic exposure to ensure they receive proper medical attention.

retrieved_docs.title: First Degree Burn -- Etiology -- Classification Based on Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: An acid or alkali can cause chemical burns. Skin contact or oral mucosa ingestion cause an irritant effect that leads to chemical burn injury. Common household products that cause chemical burns include drain cleaners, paint thinner, and lye. Alkali burns tend to be more severe, as they lead to a liquefactive necrosis process. Acids, on the other hand, cause coagulative necrosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Chemical Burns -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis depends on the type of chemical and extent of the injury. Most small lesions heal well, but larger wounds often do not heal and can develop into scars. Hydrofluoric acid burns have typically been associated with loss of digits. Chemical injuries to the eye are the most serious, resulting in severe scarring and permanent loss of vision.

retrieved_docs.title: Chemical Burns -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Chemical burns have the potential to impair short and long-term health and, especially when the eye or esophagus are involved, severely alter the individual's well-being. The clinician must be vigilant to monitor even minor appearing burns, especially with HF acid, as what initially appears to be minor may have serious side effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrofluoric acid is comprised of a diatomic compound of hydrogen and fluoride atoms as a gas, while in the liquid state it has strong hydrogen bonds between the chains forming a polymeric compound. [6] Anhydrous hydrofluoric acid and hydrofluoric acid in aqueous solutions range in appearance from colorless to slightly tinted based upon the concentration and impurities.  It has a boiling point of 20 degrees celcius (68 degrees fahrenheit) at 760 mmHg, vapour density greater than air and readily dissolves in water that when diluted (exothermal reaction) is visibly indistinguishable from water.  Hydrofluoric acid has a disagreeable, pungent odor at concentrations of 0.04 ppm which is way below the OSHA PEL (Permissible Exposure Limit) of 3 ppm and is extremely corrosive and has the ability to dissovle a number of materials especially oxides. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Chemical Burns -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The most common findings represent structural changes to the tissue directly affected, for example, the eye, oral mucosa, skin, esophagus, and lower intestinal system, especially the stomach and pylorus, respiratory system, among others. In children, ingestion is generally the most worrisome event, because of changes, both short-term and long-term, often leading to extensive tissue death. Eye exposure, either acid or alkali, represents a significant acute injury. Copious irrigation is necessary, and measuring pH is appropriate, although rarely informative.

retrieved_docs.title: Hydrofluoric Acid Burns -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the pathophysiology of hydrofluoric acid burns. Outline the typical presentation of a patient with hydrofluoric acid burns. Summarize the epidemiology of hydrofluoric acid burns. Summarize the importance of collaboration and communication among the interprofessional team to enhance delivery of care for the patients affected by the hydrofluoric acid burns. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Burn Evaluation and Management(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Burns may be caused by: Abuse Chemicals such as strong acids, lye, paint thinner, or gasoline Electric currents Fire Hot liquid Hot metal, glass, or other objects Steam Radiation from X-rays Sunlight or ultraviolet light

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2089
retrieved_docs.content: Burns are medical injuries that have a direct relationship with حرق حراري, indicating thermal burns, and are commonly associated with جرح حروق, which refers to burn wounds; recognizing these connections is essential for accurate clinical assessment and effective management of burn-related injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: Burns, referred to in Arabic as حرق حراري, denote thermal injuries to the skin and underlying tissues; the term حرق حراري directly describes the heat‑induced nature of these lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1306
retrieved_docs.content: Burns represent a broad category of tissue injury that includes both thermal and chemical mechanisms. The general term “جرح حروق” (burn wound) serves as an overarching concept that encompasses specific subtypes such as “حرق من سائل” (burn from liquid), which is considered a child of the burn wound category, and “حرق حراري” (thermal burn), which is directly related to the concept of burns. The combined category “Burns and corrosions” is linked to “جرح حروق,” indicating that corrosive injuries share common features with burn wounds. Within this broader group, “Burn of head AND/OR neck” is identified as a narrower, more specific subset, highlighting that injuries to the head or neck region constitute a particular form of burns and corrosions. Overall, burns are associated with the general concept of “جرح حروق,” and recognizing these hierarchical and relational links—ranging from general burn wounds to specific types like liquid‑induced burns, thermal burns, and region‑specific injuries—facilitates more precise diagnosis, classification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1876
retrieved_docs.content: Preparations for treatment of wounds and ulcers are classified as cicatrants, meaning they are agents that promote tissue repair and scar formation, and are used to enhance the healing process of wounds and ulcerative lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS. The broader category PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS includes CICATRICANTS, which are agents that promote scar formation and facilitate wound healing. Consequently, enzymes used in wound and ulcer therapy belong to the larger group of cicatricant preparations, highlighting their role within the spectrum of therapeutic agents aimed at promoting tissue repair.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: burn), reflecting that certain fire‑related burns may involve liquid components such as hot liquids or chemicals. This dual classification acknowledges the variability in burn mechanisms and supports comprehensive documentation and treatment planning. The hierarchical relationship among burn concepts is further detailed: جرح حروق (burn wound) has a child entity حرق من سائل (liquid burn), and the general term “burns” is linked to حرق حراري (thermal burn). Additionally, burns are associated with جرح حروق, emphasizing that any burn injury inherently involves a burn wound. Procedural care for burn injuries includes debridement of wound; infection or burn, which is directly related to جرح حروق. This debridement procedure is primarily mapped from بضع قشرة الجرح (wound dressing or removal of wound crust), indicating that proper wound dressing is a foundational step before or alongside debridement in burn management. Surgical specialties intersect with these wound‑care processes. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 821
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, the concept “burns” is closely linked to various types of injury. The relationship labeled “associated_with” connects “burns” to the Arabic term “جرح حروق” (burn wound), indicating that burn wounds are a primary manifestation of burns. Additionally, “burns” maintains a “has relationship” link with “حرق حراري” (thermal burn), specifying that exposure to heat is a common cause of burn injuries. The hierarchical notion expressed by “has child” ties the term “جرح حروق” to “حرق من سائل” (liquid burn), denoting that liquid burns are a subcategory or child type of burn wound. Together, these connections illustrate that burn injuries encompass thermal and liquid mechanisms, with burn wounds serving as the clinical presentation, and the relational descriptors “has child,” “has relationship,” and “associated_with” clarifying the structural and causal links among these concepts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2182
retrieved_docs.content: Burns and corrosions is a broad classification of injury that includes جرح حروق (burn wound). Within جرح حروق there is a more specific child category حرق من سائل (burn caused by liquid). Additionally, Burn of head AND/OR neck is recognized as a narrower, more specific condition that falls under the overall category of Burns and corrosions. This hierarchical relationship clarifies how general burn and corrosion injuries relate to particular types of burn wounds and localized head or neck burns, aiding clinicians in accurate diagnosis and appropriate patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Preparations containing sulfur that are used as anti‑acne topical agents are classified as members of the mesulfen group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is classified as a traumatic event, and traumatic events are recognized as causes of burn wounds (جرح حروق).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2604
retrieved_docs.content: Burn by fire is regarded as a type of thermal burn (حرق حراري) and is classified as a burn caused by liquid (حرق من سائل), indicating that injuries resulting from fire are grouped together with those caused by hot liquids under the broader category of thermal burns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1639
retrieved_docs.content: Dermatological Agents [TC] is a therapeutic category that includes distinct therapeutic classes such as haloprogin and selenium sulfide. Both haloprogin and selenium sulfide are identified as therapeutic classes within Dermatological Agents [TC], reflecting the variety of agents used to manage skin disorders under this broader dermatological classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1169
retrieved_docs.content: Burn of head AND/OR neck is recognized as a specific, narrower form of injury within the broader category of Burns and corrosions. The Arabic term جرح حروق refers to burn wounds and is directly linked to the concept of burns; burns are associated with جرح حروق. Consequently, the head and neck burn subtype falls under the general classification of Burns and corrosions and is connected to the Arabic designation for burn injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1605
retrieved_docs.content: Burn by fire is recognized as a traumatic event that can cause a burn wound (جرح حروق). The traumatic event relationship indicates that such events are a cause of burn wounds. Burn wounds (جرح حروق) are organized within a classification system of burns, and one specific subtype within this system is liquid burn (حرق من سائل). Accordingly, Burn by fire is classified among the broader categories of burns, which also encompass liquid burns. The hierarchical “has child” relationship shows that the general category of burn wound (جرح حروق) includes more specific child categories such as liquid burn (حرق من سائل). Understanding that burns can arise from fire trauma and be grouped with other mechanisms like liquid exposure aids clinicians in accurately diagnosing, classifying, and managing burn injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 504
retrieved_docs.content: Burn by fire is identified as a traumatic event that can cause جرح حروق (burn wound). In medical taxonomy Burn by fire is classified as حرق من سائل (liquid burn) and is also linked to حرق حراري (thermal burn) through an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that fire‑related injuries are a subset of thermal burns while some classification schemes place them under liquid burns. The broader category جرح حروق (burn wound) includes حرق من سائل as a child concept, showing that liquid burns are a specific type of burn wound. The general concept of burns is associated with جرح حروق and has a relationship with حرق حراري, emphasizing that burns overall are connected to thermal injury mechanisms. Together, these interrelationships illustrate how fire‑induced injuries, burn wounds, liquid burns, and thermal burns are interconnected within the hierarchy of traumatic injuries, underscoring the importance of precise classification for accurate diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 797
retrieved_docs.content: particular bones) are nested under a universal category of late effects, which also encompasses non‑fracture injuries such as burns and frostbite. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in systematically assessing and managing the long‑term sequelae of diverse traumatic injuries.
